html:
The symbol of R'hllor, a fiery heart.

R'hllor, also known as the Lord of Light, the Heart of Fire, the God of Flame and Shadow,[1] is a prominent god in Essos, but has only a few followers in Westeros, where he is more commonly known as the red god.[2][3] The symbol of R'hllor the Red[4] is a fiery heart.[5] Red priests lead prayers to R'hllor at red temples[6] and at nightfires.[7]

R'hllor[ ]

The Lord of Light, by Dejan Delic ©

The religion is based on a dualistic, manichean view of the world: R'hllor, the god of light, heat, and life, and R'hllor's antithesis the Great Other, the god of ice and death.[8] They are locked in an eternal struggle over the fate of the world; a struggle that, according the ancient prophecies from the books of Asshai, will only end when Azor Ahai, the messianic figure, returns wielding a flaming sword called Lightbringer, the Red Sword of Heroes, and raises dragons from stone.[8]

R’hllor is also called the Lord of Light, the Heart of Fire, the God of Flame and Shadow.[1][8] His nemesis, the Great Other, whose name may not be spoken, is known as the Lord of Darkness, the Soul of Ice, the God of Night and Terror.[8][9]

The red priests of R'hllor associate fire with life. They see shadows as creations of the Lord of Light, servants of light and the children of fire.[10] According to the priestess Melisandre, the Lord of Light cherishes innocent and beautiful people and items, making them the most precious sacrifice.[7][11] Melisandre is willing to burn people in the name of her god.[12][13]

The religion of the Lord of Light is found primarily in Essos.[5] R'hllor is worshipped in Asshai,[14][15] and red temples can be found in most of the Free Cities, including Braavos,[16][17] Lys,[7] Myr,[18] Pentos,[19] Qohor,[20] Tyrosh,[21][22] and Volantis.[23] The town of Selhorys, ruled by Volantis,[23] contains a red temple as well.[6] The Temple of the Lord of Light in Volantis is exceptionally large, said to be the greatest in all the world. According to Archmaester Gramyon, it is about three times the size of the Great Sept of Baelor in King's Landing.[23]

There are few followers of the Lord of Light in Westeros.[5] Red priests can be found in Dorne,[24] Dragonstone,[1] King's Landing,[25] and Oldtown.[26]

Organization[ ]

Priests[ ]

Red priests are taught prayers and spells, and are trained to see visions in fires. It takes many years of training to see the shapes beyond the flames, and even more years to learn how to interpret them.[18][27] Numerous priests, both male and female, are trained in their red temples. The priests of the Lord of Light wear loose red robes.[25][1][7][28]

Temple of the Lord of Light[ ]

A fanatical follower of R'hllor, by Anna Mohrbacher © Fantasy Flight Games

The Temple of the Lord of Light in Volantis has been called the greatest temple in the known world.[23] All those who serve there are slaves who were bought as children and trained.[29] Whether this is also the case at temples in other cities is unknown. Thoros, a priest from Myr, states he was given over to the red temple as a young child.[18]

Benerro, the High Priest in Volantis, also holds the titles Flame of Truth, Light of Wisdom, First Servant of the Lord of Light, and Slave of R'hllor.[6] As is the custom of that city, the red priests of the Volantene temple, all slaves, have flames tattooed across their cheeks, chin and forehead.[29][30][31][32] Red priests from other places, such as Thoros or Melisandre, are not known to have such tattoos.

The Fiery Hand are a thousand slave soldiers who guard the Temple of the Lord of Light.[33] The Volantene temple also trains some of their slaves to become temple prostitutes.[23][29]

Many worshippers in Volantis pin scraps of red cloth to their sleeves or tie them around their brow.[29]

Practices[ ]

Prayers[ ]

Red priestess at a nightfire, by Jen Zee ©

Followers of R'hllor pray before a nightfire, thanking R'hllor for ending the day, while beseeching him to bring the dawn and banish the darkness.[27] The nightfires are lit at sunset, and the prayers continue for more than an hour afterwards.[34][27] Fires are also lit each morning at first light, where the red priests welcome the sun.[26] Other priests might attend to their fires during the entire night.[30] A common phrase of prayer is "the night is dark and full of terrors".[1][26]

Public prayers to R'hllor are typically in a call-and-response style, led by the red priest and responded by the assembled worshippers. An example of a prayer sequence is:

Call: Lead us from the darkness, O my Lord. Fill our hearts with fire, so we may walk your shining path. R'hllor, you are the light in our eyes, the fire in our hearts, the heat in our loins. Yours is the sun that warms our days, yours the stars that guard us in the dark of night.
Response: Lord of Light, defend us. The night is dark and full of terrors. Lord of Light, protect us.
Call: R'hllor who gave us breath, we thank you. R'hllor who gave us day, we thank you.
Response: We thank you for the sun that warms us. We thank you for the stars that watch us. We thank you for our hearths and for our torches, that keep the savage dark at bay.[27]

Red priests might also pray before a trial by combat:

Call: Lord of Light, look down upon us.
Response: Lord of Light, defend us.
Call: Lord of Light, protect us in the darkness.
Response: Lord of Light, shine your face upon us.
Call: Light your flame among us, R'hllor. Show us the truth or falseness of this man. Strike him down if he is guilty, and give strength to his sword if he is true. Lord of Light, give us wisdom.
Response: For the night is dark and full of terrors.[35]

Marriage customs[ ]

Worshippers of R'hllor, by David A. Nash © Fantasy Flight Games

During one example of a wedding ceremony, a red priest or priestess recites ceremonial prayers, which are answered by the wedding guests. The priest and groom await the bride by a ditchfire. The bride is escorted to the priest, who first asks the bride to identify herself, and next asks who comes to claim the bride. Both bride and groom are asked whether they will share their fire with their spouse-to-be, to warm him/her "when the night is dark and full of terrors". Bride and groom then leap over the ditchfire together, to emerge "as one". Following this, the groom removes the bride's maiden's cloak and places the bride's cloak around her shoulders.[N 1] Following the ceremony is the wedding feast.[36]

Death[ ]

Priests of R'hllor believe that "life is warmth, and warmth is fire, and fire is God's and God's alone."[37] When a person has died, he is said to have "ascended to the Hall of Light, to sit beside the Lord".[11]

The death rite administered by priests to the deceased is known as the last kiss. The priest fills his mouth with fire and breaths the flames inside the deceased person, down his throat to his lungs, heart, and soul. All red priests are required to perform the rite.[37]

Visions and Magic[ ]

Worshippers asking R'hllor to bring back the dawn, by Magali Villeneuve © Fantasy Flight Games

Red priests are trained to see visions in the flames of their nightfires. These visions could be about the past, the future, or things happening far away from the location of the priest.[18] However, visions cannot always be seen in the flames,[38][18][7] and even though priests believe their visions are never wrong,[8] it is not always easy to see or interpret the vision.[2][39][36]

It takes years of training to see the shapes beyond the flames, and even longer to learn to distinguish visions about the past from visions about a certain future and a possible future.[27] Even after such long training, it remains difficult,[27] and priests might still err in their interpretation.[11][36] Many priests have been brought down by such incorrect interpretations.[39]

R'hllor is also said to give his priests the power to see through falsehoods, as "the Other's servants oft hide black hearts in gaudy light".[8]

The priestess Melisandre claims that shadows are servants of light and children of fire.[10] She does not feel the need to eat food, believing that R'hllor provides her with all the nourishment she needs.[39] Melisandre claims that glamors are given by the Lord of Light to his servants for them to use.[39] It is unknown if all of Melisandre's beliefs and abilities are solely due to her service to R'hllor, or because of her training as a shadowbinder.

History[ ]

Followers of R'hllor believe that he made the sun and the stars.[6]

A prophecy found in ancient books of Asshai claims that after a long summer an evil cold darkness shall fall on the world. Azor Ahai, wielding the sword Lightbringer, shall be reborn to combat this darkness.[7]

In an attempt to gain more followers in Westeros, the red priest Thoros of Myr was sent to the royal court at King's Landing to convert the fire-obsessed King Aerys II Targaryen. Thoros was unsuccessful, however, and started to question his own faith.[18] Thoros remained in King's Landing, and as such was present during the immediate aftermath of the Sack of King’s Landing.[35] He remained at court during the reign of King Robert I Baratheon, becoming the king's frequent drinking companion.[18]

The red priestess Melisandre travels to Dragonstone on her own initiative[40] in search of the prophesied prince Azor Ahai.[41] She is convinced that Dragonstone is the place of smoke and salt amidst which Azor Ahai shall be reborn.[8][42] She comes to believe that Stannis Baratheon, the Lord of Dragonstone, is Azor Ahai reborn,[41] and eventually becomes a confidant to Stannis's wife, Selyse Florent.[43]

Recent Events[ ]

A Game of Thrones[ ]

Illyrio Mopatis tells Daenerys Targaryen that the red priests have promised that the Lord of Light would hold Pentos's walls against a million Dothraki.[34]

A Clash of Kings[ ]

Melisandre of Asshai, by Christine Griffin © Fantasy Flight Games

At Dragonstone, Melisandre survives a poisoning attempt by Maester Cressen, claiming that it was the power of R'hllor, and that "fire cleanses".[1] The red priestess convinces Stannis Baratheon to claim the mantle of Azor Ahai and wield Lightbringer. Followers of R'hllor at Stannis's court are known as queen's men.[7] The painted statues of the Seven from the sept at Dragonstone are burned outside the castle.[7]

Melisandre, who is also a shadowbinder, births shadow assassins to kill Lord Renly Baratheon[44] and Ser Cortnay Penrose[45] at the siege of Storm's End, telling Ser Davos Seaworth that shadows are servants of light.[10] The priestess has Stannis burn the godswood at Storm's End[46] as another sacrifice to R'hllor.[47]

A Storm of Swords[ ]

Ser Gerald Gower, one of the king's men, believes R'hllor is a feeble god after Stannis's defeat in the Battle of the Blackwater.[11] Melisandre and Selyse Florent, Stannis's wife, burn Lord Guncer Sunglass and the surviving sons of Ser Hubard Rambton for treason at Dragonstone, sacrificing them to the Lord of Light.[12][8] Melisandre wants to sacrifice Edric Storm to R'hllor, believing that doing so would awaken a stone dragon at Dragonstone.[2][11] Stannis refuses her, and instead three leeches of the boy's blood are ritually burned to curse the three rival kings, Joffrey Baratheon, Balon Greyjoy, and Robb Stark.[2] Subsequently, Balon falls at Pyke,[48] Robb is killed in the Red Wedding[49] — which Ser Axell Florent attributes to the hand of R'hllor[11] — and Joffrey is poisoned at his royal wedding.[50] After Joffrey's death, Davos instead has Edric smuggled away from the island to prevent him from being sacrificed, and convinces Stannis that the true enemy is at the Wall.[27]

Arya Stark, Arya's companion Gendry, and the outlaw Sandor Clegane are captured by the brotherhood without banners.[51][52] Sandor fights a trial by combat against Lord Beric Dondarrion, who fights with a sword that he lights on fire by slicing his hand open and letting the blood wash over the blade. Despite Sandor's fear of fire, he is able to slay Beric, cleaving him from shoulder to breastbone. However, Beric is revived by Thoros, much to the shock of the captives.[35] Thoros of Myr explains to Arya Stark that he first resurrected Beric while performing the last kiss funeral rite after the battle at the Mummer's Ford, attributing Beric's miraculous revival to R'hllor.[37] Many of the brotherhood now worship the Lord of Light,[35] and Beric performs knighting ceremonies that no longer mention the Seven.[37]

Following the battle beneath the Wall, where Stannis and his men defeated an attack on the Night's Watch of free folk led by Mance Rayder, the blind maester Aemon asks to see Lightbringer, and Samwell Tarly describes the sword to him.[53] Stannis offers to name Jon Snow as Lord of Winterfell. Jon declines, however, as Melisandre would require him to burn the heart tree in the godswood of Winterfell.[9][54]

A Feast for Crows[ ]

Melisandre prays at a nightfire, by Drazenka Kimpel © Fantasy Flight Games

In Qohor, the followers of R'hllor try to burn the Black Goat.[20]

Ser Daven Lannister informs Ser Jaime Lannister that his scouts have spotted nightfires in the high places and villages near the siege of Riverrun.[55]

Maester Aemon tells Samwell Tarly that Stannis's Lightbringer is wrong, an empty glamor, which Melisandre must know. Aemon states that Stannis is not the true Azor Ahai reborn or prince that was promised, but Daenerys Targaryen is.[56]

A Dance with Dragons[ ]

Davos recalls that Melisandre had sacrificed Lord Alester Florent, who had been accused of treason, to R'hllor, in order to have strong winds for Stannis's voyage north to Eastwatch-by-the-Sea.[57] Free folk who have surrendered to Stannis are required by Melisandre to burn weirwood branches.[58] Jon Snow, now Lord Commander of the Night's Watch, observes at Castle Black that Stannis's sword, which is claimed to be Lightbringer, is cold instead of warm.[58] Melisandre believes that her power is stronger at the Wall than it had been in Asshai.[39]

Stannis's army is slowed by a blizzard during their march on Winterfell. Some of the queen's men desire to sacrifice Asha Greyjoy, a follower of the Drowned God, so that R'hllor will end the storm.[59][60] Four Peasebury men are burned after being accused of cannibalism.[60]

In Volantis, Tyrion Lannister witnesses Benerro preaching at the Temple of the Lord of Light that Daenerys Targaryen is Azor Ahai reborn and encouraging many to support her.[29] The High Priest sends Moqorro to convert the Queen of Meereen.[30]

After Moqorro heals the wounded hand of Victarion Greyjoy, the Lord Captain of the Iron Fleet decides to honor R'hllor in addition to his Drowned God. Victarion sacrifices slave girls by burning them alive on a fishing ketch set adrift to burn and then sink in Slaver's Bay.[32]

According to the High Sparrow, the new High Septon, Stannis has turned from the truth of the Seven to worship a red demon, and his false faith has no place in the Seven Kingdoms.[61]

Known red priests[ ]

Benerro, by mustamirri ©
  • Benerro, High Priest at the Temple of the Lord of Light in Volantis
  • Ezzelyno
  • Melisandre of Asshai
  • Moqorro
  • Thoros of Myr

Quotes by believers[ ]

Axell: The Lady Melisandre tells us that sometimes R'hllor permits his faithful servants to glimpse the future in flames. It seemed to me as I watched the fire this morning that I was looking at a dozen beautiful dancers, maidens garbed in yellow silk spinning and swirling before a great king. I think it was a true vision, ser. A glimpse of the glory that awaits His Grace after we take King's Landing and the throne that is his by rights.
Davos: I saw only fire, but the smoke was making my eyes water.[7]

—Axell Florent and Davos Seaworth

R'hllor is the source of all good.[8]

—Melisandre to Davos Seaworth

It was not me who raised him, my lady. It was the Lord. R'hllor is not done with him yet. Life is warmth, and warmth is fire, and fire is God's and God's alone.[37]

—Thoros to Arya Stark

R'hllor chooses such instruments as he requires. His ways are mysterious, but no man may withstand his fiery will.[11]

—Melisandre to Stannis Baratheon

The Lord of Light in his wisdom made us male and female, two parts of a greater whole. In our joining there is power. Power to make life. Power to make light. Power to cast shadows.[62]

—Melisandre to Jon Snow

R'hllor spoke to his chosen ones through blessed fire, in a language of ash and cinder and twisting flame that only a god could truly grasp.[39]

—Melisandre's thoughts

Victarion: Your red god will have his due, but the seas are ruled by the Drowned God.
Moqorro: There are no gods but R'hllor and the Other, whose name may not be said.[32]

—Victarion Greyjoy and Moqorro

Quotes by non-believers[ ]

May the Others bugger your Lord of Light.[5]

—Cortnay Penrose to Melisandre

The night is dark and full of turnips. Let us all pray for venison, my children, with some onions and a bit of tasty gravy.[58]

—Pyp, to his fellow watchmen

Light our fire and protect us from the dark, blah, blah, light our way and keep us toasty warm, the night is full of terrors, save us from the scary thing, and blah blah blah some more.[30]

—thoughts of Tyrion Lannister

Red Rahloo means nothing here.[60]

—Hugo Wull to Godry Farring

Corliss: You northmen brought these snows upon us. You and your demon trees. R'hllor will save us.
Artos: R'hllor will doom us.[60]

—Corliss Penny and Artos Flint

The red god's choir only knows a single song.[63]

—Asha Greyjoy to Stannis Baratheon, regarding their demand that Theon Greyjoy be sacrificed

Behind the Scenes[ ]

According to George R. R. Martin, the cult of R'hllor is inspired by the dualistic religious doctrines of Zoroastrianism and Catharism.[64]

Notes[ ]

  1. ↑ It is possible the cloaking sequence is not part of the original R'hlloric ceremony, but a concession to Westerosi marriage custom, as the only wedding done under the faith of R'hllor described so far is the one between Sigorn, a Thenn, and Alys Karstark, a noble northwoman.[36]
